After VR training, how Missouri State University’s Soft Ball Team Performs

Shekhar Dash

2026-05-01

#I have processed raw data sets to create two clean datasets:

cleaned_vr_sessions.csv: This contains every individual score recorded in the VR coaching form, expanded so that entries with multiple scores (e.g., “29,30,33”) are treated as sequential sessions.

batting_averages.csv: This contains the batting averages extracted from the season statistics.

library(tidyverse)
## ── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
## ✔ dplyr     1.1.4     ✔ readr     2.1.6
## ✔ forcats   1.0.1     ✔ stringr   1.6.0
## ✔ ggplot2   4.0.1     ✔ tibble    3.3.0
## ✔ lubridate 1.9.4     ✔ tidyr     1.3.2
## ✔ purrr     1.2.0     
## ── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
## ✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
## ✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
## ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors
library(knitr)

vr_data <- read_csv("cleaned_vr_sessions.csv")
## Rows: 355 Columns: 4
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Delimiter: ","
## chr  (1): Name
## dbl  (2): Score, Session
## dttm (1): Timestamp
## 
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
## ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
stats_data <- read_csv("cleaned_batting_stats.csv")
## Rows: 8 Columns: 4
## ── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
## Delimiter: ","
## chr (3): Last, First, FullName
## dbl (1): AVG
## 
## ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
## ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.
vr_data <- vr_data %>%
  mutate(Name = case_when(
    str_detect(tolower(Name), "emily gerardy") ~ "Emily Gerardy",
    str_detect(tolower(Name), "emily chadwick") ~ "Emily Chadwick",
    str_detect(tolower(Name), "emily evans") ~ "Emily Evans",
    str_detect(tolower(Name), "alexis") ~ "Alexis Perales",
    str_detect(tolower(Name), "brynn") ~ "Brynn Romine",
    str_detect(tolower(Name), "koranda") ~ "Emily Koranda",
    str_detect(tolower(Name), "^eb$|elizabeth") ~ "Elizabeth Bobbitt",
    str_detect(tolower(Name), "mya") ~ "Mya January",
    str_detect(tolower(Name), "kayla") ~ "Kayla Ulrich",
    str_detect(tolower(Name), "halle") ~ "Halle Studer",
    str_detect(tolower(Name), "lauren") ~ "Lauren Rowland",
    str_detect(tolower(Name), "sidney") ~ "Sidney McLaughlin",
    str_detect(tolower(Name), "paityn") ~ "Paityn Engemann",
    str_detect(tolower(Name), "nicole") ~ "Nicole Norman",
    TRUE ~ Name
  ))
# 1. DATA PREPARATION: Calculate Improvement and Filter First/Last
# We extract the first and last score for each player
player_change <- vr_data %>%
  group_by(Name) %>%
  summarise(
    First_Score = first(Score),
    Last_Score = last(Score),
    Sessions_Count = n()
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    Improvement_Pct = ((Last_Score - First_Score) / First_Score) * 100
  )

# Prepare for Slopegraph
slope_data <- player_change %>%
  select(Name, First_Score, Last_Score) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(First_Score, Last_Score), 
               names_to = "Session_Type", 
               values_to = "Score") %>%
  mutate(Session_Type = factor(Session_Type, levels = c("First_Score", "Last_Score")))

# 2. MATCHING: Merge VR improvement with Batting Averages
# Note: We use "fuzzy" logic or specific mapping for names like 'Lauren'
merged_data <- player_change %>%
  mutate(MatchName = case_when(
    Name == "Lauren" ~ "Lauren Rowland",
    Name == "Makenzi Swick" ~ "Makenzi Swick",
    Name == "Alexis Perales" ~ "Alexis Perales",
    TRUE ~ Name
  )) %>%
  inner_join(stats_data, by = c("MatchName" = "FullName"))
ggplot(slope_data, aes(x = Session_Type, y = Score, group = Name)) +
  geom_line(aes(color = Name), linewidth = 1, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_point(aes(color = Name), size = 3) +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = c("First Session", "Latest Session"), expand = c(0.2, 0.2)) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Player Progress: First vs. Last VR Session",
       subtitle = "Lines indicate individual score growth",
       y = "VR Score", x = "") +
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  geom_text(data = slope_data %>% filter(Session_Type == "Last_Score"),
            aes(label = Name), hjust = -0.1, size = 3)

#The Slopegraph (Lower is better)

This visualization focuses on individual trajectory. A steep downward line indicates a player who has made significant cognitive gains, their score dropped sharply, meaning their visual system is processing the ball faster.

ggplot(merged_data, aes(x = Improvement_Pct, y = AVG)) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "gray", linetype = "dashed", se = T) +
  geom_point(size = 4, color = "darkred") +
  geom_text(aes(label = Name), vjust = -1, size = 3) +
  theme_bw() +
  labs(title = "VR Improvement vs. Batting Average",
       x = "VR Score Improvement (%)",
       y = "Batting Average (AVG)") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::number_format(accuracy = 0.001))
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

#correlation analysis You are looking at a “correlation.” If the points generally move from the bottom-left to the top-right, it provides evidence that players who show the most growth in their VR training also tend to have higher batting averages on the field.

ggplot(vr_data, aes(x = factor(Session), y = Name, fill = Score)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white") +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#084594", high = "#f7fbff",
                    name = "VR Score\n(lower = better)") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Team VR Performance Heatmap",
       x = "Session Number",
       y = "Player Name",
       fill = "Score")

#The Team Heatmap

Consistency: It shows you who is completing their reps.

Team Trends: If you see a specific session number where almost everyone’s score dropped, it might indicate a week where the team was fatigued or the training environment was distracting.

Intensity: The color gradients allow you to quickly identify your “high-performers” (darker cells) versus those who may need more focused coaching (lighter cells).
